Fraud detection further amplifies the advantage: card organizations run models covering billions of transactions in real time, while stablecoins currently lack a comparable network-level anti-fraud layer. Small payments are often considered a weakness of bank cards, but bank card networks have long been adapted to mismatched transaction scenarios. Visa, by aggregating daily settlements, has processed over 2 billion transportation tickets. The bank card industry has never given up on any transaction category; they always invent new products to dominate it. There's also an identity objection: "Agents cannot hold bank cards." But an agent is simply a new device. Your phone, watch, and computer all hold independent tokens pointing to the same card, just like Apple Pay technology. Your phone has never undergone KYC; it simply carries your token, and the same applies to agents. Visa has issued over 16 billion tokens, and agents will also receive these tokens. Visa's Smart Commerce framework is being piloted, Mastercard Agent Pay is available to cardholders nationwide, Stripe and OpenAI's Smart Agent Commerce protocol has been integrated into Etsy, and over a million Shopify merchants are about to join. For existing merchants and consumers, bank cards are likely to dominate Smart Agent e-commerce. The opportunity for stablecoins lies elsewhere: serving merchants who don't yet exist. Every platform migration creates a new batch of merchants that existing payment systems cannot serve. When eBay was founded, individual sellers couldn't obtain merchant accounts; PayPal served them, growing to over a million users and processing 40% of eBay's auction payments by 2000. Shopify grew from 42,000 merchants to 5.5 million in 13 years. As Alex Rampell and James da Costa pointed out, when Stripe was founded, many of its future customers didn't even exist. The pattern remains consistent: winners serve merchants that existing institutions can't cover. The AI ​​wave is likely creating these merchants faster than any previous platform migration. Last year alone, 36 million new developers joined GitHub. In the YC 2025 winter batch, a quarter of the companies had over 95% of their codebases generated by AI. On the popular AI coding platform Bolt.new, 67% of its 5 million users are not developers. People who couldn't write production-ready code two years ago are now releasing software. Everyone is a new buyer of developer infrastructure, purchasing via command line rather than sales communication. These developers are also new sellers. A "climate developer's" workflow requires various services: data interfaces, testing infrastructure, and deployment tools. Anyone with the same tools can develop and sell these services. The same force is creating both buyers and sellers simultaneously. Imagine this: an ambient developer spends four hours using AI tools to create a tool that displays the financial data of a publicly traded company—no website, no terms of service, no legal entity. Another developer's AI agent calls it 40,000 times a week, earning $0.10 each time, generating $40 in revenue, without any human access to the checkout page. I see ambient developers creating these kinds of tools every week, and their first question is always: How do I collect payment? For most people, the current answer is: No. Existing payment service providers struggle to integrate these types of merchants, not because of technical limitations, but because once a payment service provider approves a merchant's application, they bear the merchant's risk. If the merchant commits fraud or generates a large number of chargebacks, the payment service provider has to cover it. Applicants who cannot be insured are directly rejected. A tool without a website, a registered entity, or records is extremely difficult to pass underwriting audits. The system functions normally as designed, but it wasn't originally designed for this type of scenario. Payment providers can adapt; they've done it before, creating new risk levels for payment service providers and platforms. But it took 16 years from PayPal's inception to the industry's first underwriting guideline for the merchant aggregator model it pioneered. And these new merchants now need to receive payments. For them, accepting stablecoins is like a street vendor only accepting cash. Not because cash is better, but because merchants with this profile have historically struggled to pass bank card acceptance audits. In this gap, stablecoins are currently the only viable option, even if the wallet experience is still rudimentary and the compliance framework is still developing. Protocols like x402 have embedded stablecoin payments directly into HTTP requests, requiring no merchant account, no payment processor, no onboarding, and no liability for chargebacks. This doesn't require anyone to acknowledge that stablecoins are better than bank cards; it only requires that existing payment providers haven't adapted yet. These merchants aren't choosing between stablecoins and bank cards; they're choosing between stablecoins and "not receiving payments." What will emerge? Every wave of new merchants will eventually be absorbed by traditional payment systems, and this time will likely be no different—it's just a matter of time. But merchants always emerge first, followed by the underwriting system. In the gap between these two moments, stablecoins are the infrastructure. Bank cards serve every merchant that payment providers can underwrite; stablecoins serve every merchant that payment providers cannot. The next wave of e-commerce will emerge from this gap.
